core_thesis: @culturalfingerprints treats NYC restaurant choking compliance signs as a form of vernacular urban folklore — each sign is a cultural artifact encoding the stories, languages, and identities of the communities that produce them. The creator draws a sustained parallel between this fieldwork and traditional folklore studies, arguing that custom signage and fairy tales are both expressions of "the people's voice." Through archiving, podcasting, and comparative analysis, the project surfaces hidden cultural meaning embedded in the everyday visual landscape of New York City.
key_ideas: Cultural fingerprinting as a framework for reading signs as diaspora/identity artifacts
NYC choking compliance signs as vernacular art and hidden urban storytelling
ATU tale-type classification system applied to cross-cultural folklore comparison
Parallel between traditional fairy tale collection and urban sign archiving as forms of folklore
Language and semiotics as encoded in bilingual/multilingual signage
Podcast as a medium for collecting oral histories from sign designers and restaurant owners
Intersection of local subculture (sports, pop culture, ethnicity) with regulatory safety design
Fieldwork and archiving methodology as research practice
